title: Defining appropriate quantities for modeling
description: Define how quantities of interest are calculated in modeling problems.

assessment_items.question_content:
Charlotte owns two entertainment websites. Here are some details about those websites for one entire month:

 | Website A | Website B
:- | :-: | :-:
Number of writers  | $5$ | $11$
Number of posts posted | $110$ | $200$
Average number of words of post | $100$ | $170$
Average likes per post | $3500$ | $3500$
Average comments per post | $500$ | $450$
Number of new subscribers | $9000$ | $20{,}000$
Revenue | $\$30{,}000$ | $\$75{,}000$
Expenses | $\$20{,}000$ | $\$47{,}000$
Profit (Revenue$-$Expenses) | $\$10{,}000$ | $\$28{,}000$

Charlotte wants to know which website has more user engagement per a single writer.

**1) Charlotte thought of two different ways to define this quantity. Identify these two definitions among the following options.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**2) Determine which website has more user engagement per a single writer according to the two definitions. Did you get the same result for both definitions? **

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that there isn't a single way to define "engagement." The number of likes can be a good measure of that. The number of comments is also a good measure. The numbers of views or shares can also be good measures, but we are not given information about that.

Now let's go over the options and see which of them might be used to describe "user engagement per a single writer."

Definition | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-:
Average number of likes per writer | "Number of likes" is a measure of user engagement. Taking the average per writer makes a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Total number of comments | "Total number of comments" is a measure of user engagement, but without taking the average per writer, this is not a good definition. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Average number of words per writer |  "Number of words" isn't a measure for user engagement. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Average number of comments per writer | "Number of comments" is a measure of user engagement. Taking the average per writer makes a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$

We are left with two definitions for user engagement per a single writer:

1. Average number of likes per writer
2. Average number of comments per writer

Let's calculate the user engagement per a single writer for each website according to each definition:

Website | Definition #1 | Definition #2
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $77{,}000\,\dfrac{\text{likes}}{\text{writer}}$ | $11{,}000\,\dfrac{\text{comments}}{\text{writer}}$
B | $63{,}636.\overline{36}\,\dfrac{\text{likes}}{\text{writer}}$ | $8181.\overline{81}\,\dfrac{\text{comments}}{\text{writer}}$
Which website has more user engagement per a single writer? | Website A | Website A

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In conclusion:

1) These are the two definitions Charlotte came up with to define the quantity:

- Average number of likes per writer
- Average number of comments per writer

2) According to both definitions, website A has more user engagement per a single writer.

choices.content: Average number of likes per writer
Total number of comments
Average number of words per writer
Average number of comments per writer
Yes. According to both definitions, website A has more user engagement per a single writer.
Yes. According to both definitions, website B has more user engagement per a single writer.
No. The definitions have opposite results.

assessment_items.question_content:
Charlotte owns two entertainment websites. Here are some details about those websites for one entire month:

 | Website A | Website B
:- | :-: | :-:
Number of writers  | $5$ | $11$
Number of posts posted | $110$ | $200$
Average number of words of post | $100$ | $170$
Average likes per post | $3500$ | $3500$
Average comments per post | $500$ | $450$
Number of new subscribers | $9000$ | $20{,}000$
Revenue | $\$30{,}000$ | $\$75{,}000$
Expenses | $\$20{,}000$ | $\$47{,}000$
Profit (Revenue$-$Expenses) | $\$10{,}000$ | $\$28{,}000$

Charlotte wants to know which website has more user engagement per a single dollar expense.

**1) Charlotte thought of two different ways to define this quantity. Identify these two definitions among the following options.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**2) Determine which website has more user engagement per a single dollar expense according to the two definitions. Did you get the same result for both definitions? **

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that there isn't a single way to define "engagement." The number of likes can be a good measure of that. The number of comments is also a good measure. The numbers of views or shares can also be good measures, but we are not given information about that.

Now let's go over the options and see which of them might be used to describe "user engagement per a single dollar expense."

Definition | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-:
Total number of likes divided by total number of comments | "Total number of likes" is a measure of user engagement, but without dividing by the expenses, this is not a good definition. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Total number of comments divided by the expenses | "Total number of comments" is a measure of user engagement. Dividing this by the expenses gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Total number of comments |  "Total number of comments" is a measure of user engagement, but without dividing by the expenses, this is not a good definition. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Total number of likes divided by the expenses | "Total number of likes" is a measure of user engagement. Dividing this by the expenses gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$

We are left with two definitions for user engagement per a single dollar expense:

1. Total number of comments divided by the expenses
2. Total number of likes divided by the expenses

Let's calculate the user engagement per a single dollar for each website according to each definition:

Website | Definition #1 | Definition #2
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $2.75\,\dfrac{\text{comments}}{\text{dollar}}$ | $19.25\,\dfrac{\text{likes}}{\text{dollar}}$
B | $\approx 1.91\,\dfrac{\text{comments}}{\text{dollar}}$ | $\approx 14.89\,\dfrac{\text{likes}}{\text{dollar}}$
Which website has more user engagement per a single dollar expense? | Website A | Website A

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In conclusion:

1) These are the two definitions Charlotte came up with to define the quantity:

- Total number of comments divided by the expenses
- Total number of likes divided by the expenses

2) According to both definitions, website A has more user engagement per a single dollar expense.

choices.content: Total number of likes divided by total number of comments
Total number of comments divided by the expenses
Total number of comments
Total number of likes divided by the expenses
Yes. According to both definitions, website A has more user engagement per a single dollar expense.
Yes. According to both definitions, website B has more user engagement per a single dollar expense.
No. The definitions have opposite results.

assessment_items.question_content:
Charlotte owns two entertainment websites. Here are some details about those websites for one entire month:

 | Website A | Website B
:- | :-: | :-:
Number of writers  | $5$ | $11$
Number of posts posted | $110$ | $200$
Average number of words of post | $100$ | $170$
Average likes per post | $3500$ | $3500$
Average comments per post | $500$ | $450$
Number of new subscribers | $9000$ | $20{,}000$
Revenue | $\$30{,}000$ | $\$75{,}000$
Expenses | $\$20{,}000$ | $\$47{,}000$
Profit (Revenue$-$Expenses) | $\$10{,}000$ | $\$28{,}000$

Charlotte wants to know which website generates more profit per resource invested.

**1) Charlotte thought of two different ways to define this quantity. Identify these two definitions among the following options.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**2) Determine which website generates more profit per resource invested according to the two definitions. Did you get the same result for both definitions? **

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that there isn't a single way to define "resource invested." Expenses can be a good measure of that. The number of writers is also a good measure. The total number of hours (or days) of work can also be a good measure, but we are not given information about that.

Now let's go over the options and see which of them might be used to describe "profit per resource invested."

Definition | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-:
Profit divided by total number of likes | "Total number of likes" is not a measure of resource. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Profit divided by number of writers | "Number of writers" is a measure of resource. Dividing profit by it gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Profit divided by expenses |  "Expenses" is a measure of resource. Dividing profit by it gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Expenses divided by number of writers | "Number of writers" is a measure of resource, but without dividing profit by it, this is not a good definition. | $\redD{\text{No}}$

We are left with two definitions for profit per resource invested:

1. Profit divided by number of writers
2. Profit divided by expenses

Let's calculate the profit per resource invested for each website according to each definition:

Website | Definition #1 | Definition #2
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $2000\,\dfrac{\text{dollars profit}}{\text{writer}}$ | $0.5\,\dfrac{\text{dollars profit}}{\text{dollar expense}}$
B | $2545.\overline{45}\,\dfrac{\text{dollars profit}}{\text{writer}}$ | $\approx 0.6\,\dfrac{\text{dollars profit}}{\text{dollar expense}}$
Which website generates more profit per resource invested? | Website B | Website B

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In conclusion:

1) These are the two definitions Charlotte came up with to define the quantity:

- Profit divided by number of writers
- Profit divided by expenses

2) According to both definitions, website B generates more profit per resource invested.

choices.content: Profit divided by total number of likes
Profit divided by number of writers
Profit divided by expenses
Expenses divided by number of writers
Yes. According to both definitions, website A generates more profit per resource invested.
Yes. According to both definitions, website B generates more profit per resource invested.
No. The definitions have opposite results.

Charlotte wants to know which website produces a larger amount of content per a single writer.

**1) Charlotte thought of two different ways to define this quantity. Identify these two definitions among the following options.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**2) Determine which website produces a larger amount of content per a single writer according to the two definitions. Did you get the same result for both definitions? **

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that there isn't a single way to define "amount of content." The number of posts can be a good measure of that. The number of words is also a good measure. The amount of characters can also be a good measure, but we are not given information about that.

Now let's go over the options and see which of them might be used to describe "amount of content per a single writer."

Definition | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-:
Average number of posts per writer | "Number of posts" is a measure of content amount. Taking the average per writer gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Total number of words | "Number of words" is a measure of content amount, but without taking the average per writer, this is not a good definition. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Average number of words per writer |  "Number of words" is a measure of content amount. Taking the average per writer gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Average number of likes per word | "Number of likes" isn't a measure for content amount. | $\redD{\text{No}}$

We are left with two definitions for amount of content per a single writer:

1. Average number of posts per writer
2. Average number of words per writer

Let's calculate the amount of content per a single writer for each website according to each definition:

Website | Definition #1 | Definition #2
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $22\,\dfrac{\text{posts}}{\text{writer}}$ | $2200\,\dfrac{\text{words}}{\text{writer}}$
B | $18.\overline{18}\,\dfrac{\text{posts}}{\text{writer}}$ | $3090.\overline{90}\,\dfrac{\text{words}}{\text{writer}}$
Which website produces a larger amount of content per a single writer? | Website A | Website B

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In conclusion:

1) These are the two definitions Charlotte came up with to define the quantity:

- Average number of posts per writer
- Average number of words per writer

2) The definitions have opposite results.

choices.content: Average number of posts per writer
Total number of words
Average number of words per writer
Average number of likes per word
Yes. According to both definitions, website A produces a larger amount of content per a single writer.
Yes. According to both definitions, website B produces a larger amount of content per a single writer.
No. The definitions have opposite results.

Revenue | $\$30{,}000$ | $\$75{,}000$
Expenses | $\$20{,}000$ | $\$47{,}000$
Profit (Revenue$-$Expenses) | $\$10{,}000$ | $\$28{,}000$

Charlotte wants to know which website generates more revenue per amount of content.

**1) Charlotte thought of two different ways to define this quantity. Identify these two definitions among the following options.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**2) Determine which website generates more revenue per amount of content according to the two definitions. Did you get the same result for both definitions? **

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that there isn't a single way to define "amount of content." The number of posts can be a good measure of that. The number of words is also a good measure. The amount of characters can also be a good measure, but we are not given information about that.

Now let's go over the options and see which of them might be used to describe "revenue per amount of content."

Definition | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-:
Revenue divided by number of posts | "Number of posts" is a measure of content amount. Dividing revenue by this quantity gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Revenue divided by total number of words | "Number of words" is a measure of content amount. Dividing revenue by this quantity gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Revenue divided by number of writers |  "Number of writers" isn't a measure for content amount. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Number of new subscribers divided by total number of words | "Number of words" is a measure of content amount, but without dividing revenue by it, this is not a good definition. | $\redD{\text{No}}$

We are left with two definitions for revenue per amount of content:

1. Revenue divided by number of posts
2. Revenue divided by total number of words

Let's calculate the revenue per amount of content for each website according to each definition:

Website | Definition #1 | Definition #2
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $272.\overline{72}\,\dfrac{\text{dollars}}{\text{post}}$ | $2.\overline{72}\,\dfrac{\text{dollars}}{\text{word}}$
B | $375\,\dfrac{\text{dollars}}{\text{post}}$ | $\approx 2.2\,\dfrac{\text{dollars}}{\text{word}}$
Which website generates more revenue per amount of content? | Website B | Website A

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In conclusion:

1) These are the two definitions Charlotte came up with to define the quantity:

- Revenue divided by number of posts
- Revenue divided by total number of words

2) The definitions have opposite results.

choices.content: Revenue divided by number of posts
Revenue divided by total number of words
Revenue divided by number of writers
Number of new subscribers divided by total number of words
Yes. According to both definitions, website A generates more revenue per amount of content.
Yes. According to both definitions, website B generates more revenue per amount of content.
No. The definitions have opposite results.

Charlotte wants to know which website gains more new subscribers per amount of content.

**1) Charlotte thought of two different ways to define this quantity. Identify these two definitions among the following options.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**2) Determine which website gains more new subscribers per amount of content according to the two definitions. Did you get the same result for both definitions? **

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that there isn't a single way to define "amount of content." The number of posts can be a good measure of that. The number of words is also a good measure. The amount of characters can also be a good measure, but we are not given information about that.

Now let's go over the options and see which of them might be used to describe "new subscribers per amount of content."

Definition | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-:
Number of new subscribers divided by number of writers | "Number of writers" is not a measure of content amount. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Number of new subscribers divided by number of likes | "Number of likes" is not a measure of content amount. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Number of new subscribers divided by number of posts |  "Number of posts" is a measure of content amount. Dividing number of new subscribers by this quantity gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Number of new subscribers divided by number of words | "Number of words" is a measure of content amount. Dividing number of new subscribers by this quantity gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$

We are left with two definitions for new subscribers per amount of content:

1. Number of new subscribers divided by number of posts
2. Number of new subscribers divided by number of words

Let's calculate the new subscribers per amount of content for each website according to each definition:

Website | Definition #1 | Definition #2
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $81.\overline{81}\,\dfrac{\text{new subscribers}}{\text{post}}$ | $0.\overline{81}\,\dfrac{\text{new subscribers}}{\text{word}}$
B | $100\,\dfrac{\text{new subscribers}}{\text{post}}$ | $\approx 0.6\,\dfrac{\text{new subscribers}}{\text{word}}$
Which website gains more new subscribers per amount of content? | Website B | Website A

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In conclusion:

1) These are the two definitions Charlotte came up with to define the quantity:

- Number of new subscribers divided by number of posts
- Number of new subscribers divided by number of words

2) The definitions have opposite results.

choices.content: Number of new subscribers divided by number of writers
Number of new subscribers divided by number of likes
Number of new subscribers divided by number of posts
Number of new subscribers divided by number of words
Yes. According to both definitions, website A gains more subscribers per amount of content.
Yes. According to both definitions, website B gains more subscribers per amount of content.
No. The definitions have opposite results.

assessment_items.question_content:
Analia is a school district manager. Here are some details about two schools in her district for the last school year:

 | School A | School B
:- | :-: | :-:
Number of students | $3000$ | $4000$
Number of teachers | $190$ | $380$
Graduation rate | $86\%$ | $90\%$
Budget per student | $\$10{,}500$ | $\$10{,}000$
% of students in sports club | $62\%$ | $68\%$
Number of sports medals won | $9$ | $7$
SAT average | $1200$ | $1050$
SAT range (max-min) | $900$ | $700$

Analia wants to know which school has higher academic achievements relative to the number of teachers per student.

**1) Analia thought of two different ways to define this quantity. Identify these two definitions among the following options.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**2) Determine which school has higher academic achievements relative to the number of teachers per student, according to the two definitions. Did you get the same result for both definitions? **

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that there isn't a single way to define "academic achievement." Graduation rate can be a good measure of that. Average SAT score is also a good measure. The rate of students who are accepted into college can also be a good measure, but we are not given information about that.

Now let's go over the options and see which of them might be used to describe "academic achievements relative to the number of teachers per student."

Definition | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-:
Graduation rate divided by number of teachers | "Graduation rate" is a measure of academic achievement, but it's divided by the number of teachers, and not by the rate of teachers per student. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Graduation rate divided by teachers per student | "Graduation rate" is a measure of academic achievement. Dividing this by the rate of teacher per student gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
SAT average divided by number of teachers |  "SAT average" is a measure of academic achievement, but it's divided by the number of teachers, and not by the rate of teachers per student. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
SAT average divided by teachers per student | "SAT average" is a measure of academic achievement. Dividing this by the rate of teacher per student gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$

We are left with two definitions for academic achievements relative to the number of teachers per student:

1. Graduation rate divided by teachers per student
2. SAT average divided by teachers per student

Let's calculate the academic achievements relative to the number of teachers per student for each school according to each definition:

School | Definition #1 | Definition #2
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $\approx 1358\,\dfrac{\text{student percents}}{\text{teacher}}$ | $\approx 18{,}947\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{teacher}}$
B | $\approx 947\,\dfrac{\text{student percents}}{\text{teacher}}$ | $\approx 11{,}053\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{teacher}}$
Which school has higher academic achievements relative to the number of teachers per student? | School A | School A

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In conclusion:

1) These are the two definitions Analia came up with to define the quantity:

- Graduation rate divided by teachers per student
- SAT average divided by teachers per student

2) According to both definitions, school A has higher academic achievements relative to the number of teachers per student.

choices.content: Graduation rate divided by number of teachers
Graduation rate divided by teachers per student
SAT average divided by number of teachers
SAT average divided by teachers per student
Yes. According to both definitions, school A has higher academic achievements relative to the number of teachers per student.
Yes. According to both definitions, school B has higher academic achievements relative to the number of teachers per student.
No. The definitions have opposite results.

assessment_items.question_content:
Analia is a school district manager. Here are some details about two schools in her district for the last school year:

 | School A | School B
:- | :-: | :-:
Number of students | $3000$ | $4000$
Number of teachers | $190$ | $380$
Graduation rate | $86\%$ | $90\%$
Budget per student | $\$10{,}500$ | $\$10{,}000$
% of students in sports club | $62\%$ | $68\%$
Number of sports medals won | $9$ | $7$
SAT average | $1200$ | $1050$
SAT range (max-min) | $900$ | $700$

Analia wants to know which school has the higher SAT average relative to the resources invested per student.

**1) Analia thought of two different ways to define this quantity. Identify these two definitions among the following options.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**2) Determine which school has the higher SAT average relative to the resources invested per student, according to the two definitions. Did you get the same result for both definitions? **

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that there isn't a single way to define "resource invested." Rate of teachers per student can be a good measure of that. Budget per student is also a good measure. Rate of computers per student can also be a good measure, but we are not given information about that.

Now let's go over the options and see which of them might be used to describe "SAT average relative to the resources invested per student."

Definition | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-:
SAT average divided by number of sports medals won | "Number of sports medals won" isn't a measure of invested resource. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
SAT average divided by number of students | "Number of students" isn't a measure of invested resource. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
SAT average divided by budget per student |  "Budget per student" is a measure of invested resource. Dividing SAT average by it gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
SAT average divided by teachers per student | "Teachers per student" is a measure of invested resource. Dividing SAT average by it gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$

We are left with two definitions for SAT average relative to the resources invested per student:

1. SAT average divided by budget per student
2. SAT  average divided by teachers per student

Let's calculate the SAT average relative to the resources invested per student for each school according to each definition:

School | Definition #1 | Definition #2
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $\approx 0.114\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{dollar}}$ | $\approx 18{,}947\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{teacher}}$
B | $0.105\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{dollar}}$ | $\approx 11{,}053\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{teacher}}$
Which school has the higher SAT average relative to the resources invested per student? | School A | School A

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In conclusion:

1) These are the two definitions Analia came up with to define the quantity:

- SAT average divided by budget per student
- SAT average divided by teachers per student

2) According to both definitions, school A has the higher SAT average relative to the resources invested per student.

choices.content: SAT average divided by number of sports medals won
SAT average divided by number of students
SAT average divided by budget per student
SAT average divided by teachers per student
Yes. According to both definitions, school A has the higher SAT average relative to the resources invested per student.
Yes. According to both definitions, school B has the higher SAT average relative to the resources invested per student.
No. The definitions have opposite results.

assessment_items.question_content:
Analia is a school district manager. Here are some details about two schools in her district for the last school year:

 | School A | School B
:- | :-: | :-:
Number of students | $3000$ | $4000$
Number of teachers | $190$ | $380$
Graduation rate | $86\%$ | $90\%$
Budget per student | $\$10{,}500$ | $\$10{,}000$
% of students in sports club | $62\%$ | $68\%$
Number of sports medals won | $9$ | $7$
SAT average | $1200$ | $1050$
SAT range (max-min) | $900$ | $700$

Analia wants to know which school has the lower SAT range  relative to the resources invested per student (lower, as SAT range is a measure of inequality, which we want to minimize as much as possible).

**1) Analia thought of two different ways to define this quantity. Identify these two definitions among the following options.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**2) Determine which school has the lower SAT range relative to the resources invested per student, according to the two definitions. Did you get the same result for both definitions? **

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that there isn't a single way to define "resource invested." Rate of teachers per student can be a good measure of that. Budget per student is also a good measure. Rate of computers per student can also be a good measure, but we are not given information about that.

Now let's go over the options and see which of them might be used to describe "SAT range relative to the resources invested per student."

Definition | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-:
SAT range divided by number of students | "Number of students" isn't a measure of invested resource. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
SAT range divided by teachers per student | "Teachers per student" is a measure of invested resource. Dividing SAT range by it gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
SAT range divided by budget per student |  "Budget per student" is a measure of invested resource. Dividing SAT range by it gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
SAT range divided by SAT average | "SAT average" isn't a measure of invested resource. | $\redD{\text{No}}$

We are left with two definitions for SAT range relative to the resources invested per student:

1. SAT range divided by teachers per student
2. SAT range divided by budget per student

Let's calculate the SAT range relative to the resources invested per student for each school according to each definition:

School | Definition #1 | Definition #2
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $\approx 14{,}211\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{teacher}}$ | $\approx 0.086\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{dollar}}$
B | $\approx 7368\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{teacher}}$ | $0.07\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{dollar}}$
Which school has the lower SAT range relative to the resources invested per student? | School B | School B

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In conclusion:

1) These are the two definitions Analia came up with to define the quantity:

- SAT range divided by teachers per student
- SAT range divided by budget per student

2) According to both definitions, school B has the lower SAT range relative to the resources invested per student.

SAT range divided by teachers per student
SAT range divided by budget per student
SAT range divided by SAT average

assessment_items.question_content:
Analia is a school district manager. Here are some details about two schools in her district for the last school year:

 | School A | School B
:- | :-: | :-:
Number of students | $3000$ | $4000$
Number of teachers | $190$ | $380$
Graduation rate | $86\%$ | $90\%$
Budget per student | $\$10{,}500$ | $\$10{,}000$
% of students in sports club | $62\%$ | $68\%$
Number of sports medals won | $9$ | $7$
SAT average | $1200$ | $1050$
SAT range (max-min) | $900$ | $700$

Analia wants to know which school has higher academic achievements relative to the budget per student.

**1) Analia thought of two different ways to define this quantity. Identify these two definitions among the following options.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**2) Determine which school has higher academic achievements relative to the budget per student, according to the two definitions. Did you get the same result for both definitions? **

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that there isn't a single way to define "academic achievement." Graduation rate can be a good measure of that. Average SAT is also a good measure. The rate of students who are accepted into college can also be a good measure, but we are not given information about that.

Now let's go over the options and see which of them might be used to describe "academic achievements relative to the budget per student."

Definition | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-:
SAT average divided by budget per student | "SAT average" is a measure of academic achievement. Dividing this by the budget per student gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Graduation rate divided by budget per student | "Graduation rate" is a measure of academic achievement. Dividing this by the budget per student gives us a good definition. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
Number of sports medals won divided by budget per student |  "Number of sports medals won" isn't a measure of academic achievement. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
Graduation rate divided by number of sports medals won | "Graduation rate" is a measure of academic achievement, but it's divided by the number of sports medals won, and not by the budget per student. | $\redD{\text{No}}$

We are left with two definitions for academic achievements relative to the budget per student:

1. SAT average divided by budget per student
2. Graduation rate divided by budget per student

Let's calculate the academic achievements relative to the budget per student for each school according to each definition:

School | Definition #1 | Definition #2
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $\approx 0.114\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{dollar}}$ | $\approx 0.0082\,\dfrac{\text{student percents}}{\text{dollar}}$
B | $0.105\,\dfrac{\text{student points}}{\text{dollar}}$ | $0.009\,\dfrac{\text{student percents}}{\text{dollar}}$
Which school has higher academic achievements relative to the budget per student? | School A | School B

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In conclusion:

1) These are the two definitions Analia came up with to define the quantity:

- SAT average divided by budget per student
- Graduation rate divided by budget per student

2) The definitions have opposite results.

choices.content: SAT average divided by budget per student
Graduation rate divided by budget per student
Number of sports medals won divided by budget per student
Graduation rate divided by number of sports medals won
Yes. According to both definitions, School A has higher academic achievements relative to the budget per student.
Yes. According to both definitions, School B has higher academic achievements relative to the budget per student.
No. The definitions have opposite results.
